text: The surgeon uses their non-dominant hand to position the needle at the desired angle for suturing through the muscle, then grasps the needle with their dominant hand approximately three-quarters of the way back.
The surgeon positions the needle in the desired orientation, winds it backwards, and then unwinds it forwards through the tissue, demonstrating a suturing technique using robotic assistance.

The surgeon positions the needle on the opposite side of the muscle, grabs it three-quarters of the way back, winds it up, and then unwinds it through the tissue, demonstrating the technique from various angles.
The surgeon positions the needle at the desired location on the hernia sac, winds it up, and unwinds it through the tissue, demonstrating the technique for handling the hernia sac.
The surgeon positions the needle, grabs it about three quarters of the way back, winds it up, and unwinds it along its curve through the tissue.
The needle emerges from the top of the muscle, and the surgeon grabs the top of the suture to pull on it, tightening the suture one at a time.
The surgeon demonstrates locking the barb suture by reversing it with two or three throws, ensuring it holds securely.
The surgeon positions the needle vertically across the mesh, holding it three-quarters of the way back, and then winds and unwinds it through the tissue.

The surgeon secures the mesh to the muscle by grabbing both structures. The orientation is then changed to a horizontal position, with the needle held in place without needing to determine forehand or backhand technique.

The surgeon holds the needle in a horizontal position and winds it up and unwinds it through the tissue, making several passes without regrabbing due to holding the needle three-quarters of the way back.
